ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried shrimp category; no specific entry for wasabi mayonnaise coated deep-fried prawns with toasted cereal. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for deep-fried prawns with wasabi mayonnaise and cereal coating. A00LC (Crustaceans, cooked) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for this specific prepared seafood dish.

preparation_method: garnished with chilli padi and curry leaves

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical shared serving at a Zi Char restaurant is approximately 250 g, comprising 6–8 medium-to-large deep-fried prawns coated in toasted cereal and dressed with wasabi mayonnaise.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Shrimp / Prawn with Batter Coating — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152 adapted) and USDA Foundation Foods composite for mayonnaise-based sauce; wasabi condiment contribution estimated from USDA Branded Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for deep-fried prawns with wasabi mayonnaise and toasted cereal coating. Values are estimated by compositing: (1) USDA SR Legacy entry for breaded/battered fried shrimp (NDB 15152, ~220 kcal/100g), (2) USDA FoodData Central mayonnaise contribution (~30 g per 100 g dish, ~65 kcal contribution), and (3) toasted cereal coating contribution. Final composite per-100g values reflect a typical restaurant-style deep-fried prawn dish with rich sauce. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; battered/breaded fried seafood analogous estimate; mayonnaise GI ~40 (University of Sydney GI Database)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried prawns with wasabi mayonnaise and cereal coating. GI estimated at 48 based on: (1) battered/breaded fried seafood GI ~50 (University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for battered fish/seafood); (2) mayonnaise GI ~40 (fat-dominant condiment, University of Sydney GI Database); (3) toasted cereal coating contribution raising GI slightly. Weighted composite estimate yields GI ~48, classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (48 × 12.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 15.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~1050 mg per serving) from mayonnaise, seasoning, and frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load (~1050 mg per serving), which represents approximately 46% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and paired with low-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~15 per serving) from cereal coating and batter, combined with high fat content from deep-frying and mayonnaise
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in small portions due to its moderate glycemic load of approximately 15 per serving and high fat content (16 g/100g), which can impair postprandial glucose control; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting portion to half a serving is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (16 g/100g), elevated cholesterol (~145 mg/100g), and high sodium from deep-frying and mayonnaise-based sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish poses a high cardiovascular concern due to its combination of elevated cholesterol (~362 mg per serving), high sodium (~1050 mg per serving), and significant total fat from deep-frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence and limit to a small shared portion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Prawns with Wasabi Mayonnaise is a popular Zi Char and Malaysian-Singaporean seafood dish featuring large prawns that are coated in a seasoned batter mixed with toasted cereal (typically oats or cornflakes) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is finished with a generous drizzle or toss in wasabi-spiked mayonnaise, which delivers a creamy, pungent heat balanced by the richness of the mayo. It is garnished with sliced chilli padi for additional spice and fresh curry leaves for aromatic depth. The toasted cereal coating provides a distinctive crunch that sets this dish apart from standard battered prawns. Nutritionally, the dish is calorie-dense (~245 kcal/100g) due to deep-frying and the fat-rich mayonnaise sauce, with moderate protein (~14.5 g/100g) from the prawns and moderate carbohydrates (~12.5 g/100g) from the cereal and batter coating. Sodium content is high (~420 mg/100g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low), but the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~15), relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. This dish is commonly served as a shared main course at Zi Char restaurants and seafood houses in Singapore and Malaysia, typically priced at a premium due to the use of whole prawns.

meal_context: shared main course at Zi Char restaurant

confidence_reason: No exact entry for deep-fried prawns with wasabi mayonnaise and toasted cereal coating was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central data for battered/breaded fried shrimp, mayonnaise, and cereal coating contributions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for battered seafood and mayonnaise. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite sources used for this estimation. 2. The description in the input JSON contains a likely OCR/transcription error: 'cuTy leaves' has been interpreted as 'curry leaves', which is consistent with the dish profile. 3. The input cuisine tag 'Crab' appears to be a restaurant category tag rather than an ingredient; this dish contains prawns, not crab. 4. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains mayonnaise which may contain non-halal ingredients depending on the restaurant; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 5. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in approved sources. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 48 × carbohydrate_g per serving 31.25 g) / 100 = 15.0, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 12.5 × 2.5 = 31.25 g.
